heb.paragraph: "The earth is the LORD’s (la-YHWH), and everything in it" (v.1). Total divine ownership. Entrance requirements (vv.3–4): clean hands, pure heart, no idolatry, no false oaths. Psalm 15’s companion entrance liturgy.

panels.ctx: The creation claim (v.1) grounds the entrance requirements (vv.3–6). Because God owns everything, entering his presence requires holiness.

cross.note: "The earth is the Lord’s, and everything in it" — Paul quotes v.1.

notes.note: "The earth is the LORD’s." Ownership precedes worship. You enter HIS house.
"Clean hands and a pure heart" — the entrance requirements. Outer conduct (hands) and inner character (heart).

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

sections.header: Verses 7–10 — “Lift Up Your Heads, You Gates!”

heb.paragraph: "Who is this King of Glory (melek̅ hakkābōd)? The LORD strong and mighty, the LORD mighty in battle!" (v.8). The gates of Jerusalem (or heaven) are summoned to lift their lintels for the arriving King. Traditionally associated with the ark entering Jerusalem (2 Sam 6).

panels.ctx: The processional liturgy: gates summoned to open for the King of Glory. "Who is he?" asked twice, answered twice: the LORD strong and mighty, the LORD Almighty.

cross.note: David brings the ark to Jerusalem — the possible setting.
Christ seated "far above all rule and authority" — the ascension fulfilment.

notes.note: "Lift up your heads, you gates!" The gates are personified — they have heads to lift. The architecture responds to the King’s approach.
"The LORD Almighty — he is the King of Glory." The final identification. The question "who is this?" receives its definitive answer.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: Because God owns everything, entering his presence requires holiness…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVThe one who has clean hands and a pure heart, who does not trust in an idol or swear by a false god.ESVHe who has clean hands and a pure heart, who does not lift up his soul to what is false and does not swear deceitfully.NIVThe earth is the Lord’s, and everything in it, the world, and all who live in it;ESVThe earth is the LORD\u2019s and the fullness thereof, the world and those who dwell therein,

rec.text: ""The earth is the LORD’s." Ownership precedes worship. You enter HIS house."

rows.label: The Claim — 24:1–6
rows.text: "The earth is the LORD’s." Clean hands, pure heart. Who may ascend?

hebtext.note: "The earth is the LORD’s (la-YHWH), and everything in it" (v.1). Total divine ownership. Entrance requirements (vv.3–4): clean hands, pure heart, no idolatry, no false oaths. Psalm 15’s companion entrance liturgy.

"Who is this King of Glory (melek̅ hakkābōd)? The LORD strong and mighty, the LORD mighty in battle!" (v.8). The gates of Jerusalem (or heaven) are summoned to lift their lintels for the arriving King. Traditionally associated with the ark entering Jerusalem (2 Sam 6).

thread.text: "The earth is the Lord’s, and everything in it" — Paul quotes v.1.
David brings the ark to Jerusalem — the possible setting.
Christ seated "far above all rule and authority" — the ascension fulfilment.

themes.note: Sovereignty and Worship peak: the King of Glory enters. Holiness: entrance requires purity. Prophecy: the ascension tradition.
